You are ONE OF N PARALLEL CANDIDATE PLANNERS under the generate-N-then-judge scheme (Q100/Q101). Run in **CANDIDATE MODE**: ground yourself read-only, then EMIT a full candidate task-DAG as a single fenced ```json block as the LAST content of your reply. Write NOTHING to any ledger, emit NO status token.

**goalId:** G14 (candidateMode: true)

G14 is a DEFECT-SEEDED goal (ledgerRef defects:D65, description embeds the confirmed root cause + suggestedFix) in phase `planning` — skip clarification, plan directly.

Read the goal (fetch_item("goals", G14)) and defect D65 for the full confirmed root cause + suggestedFix, and ground yourself in the actual repo before planning. Key context (verify against source):
- Root cause: single-flow TCP collapses to ~3.67 Mbps because ~13% loss + ~1s bufferbloat originate in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer, which wanbond's DEFAULT active-backup scheduler does nothing to shape. Egress pacing/AQM is currently a WEIGHTED-only feature (internal/config/config.go:99-108, 218-223; internal/sched/weighted.go token-bucket pacer + SizePacingFromBDP). No wanbond-internal queue exists (multipath.go:2027-2035 synchronous send). Encode CPU has 40-80x headroom (ruled out).
- FIX (2 parts): (1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally CoDel-style AQM) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery, so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate. Design decision to make: wire pacing into active-backup directly vs expose a policy-independent pacing knob. (2) SECONDARY — install a TCP MSS clamp on wanbond0 for FORWARDED traffic (iptables/ip6tables mangle FORWARD -o wanbond0 TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu) and document it in deploy recipes (docs/install.md §9.2, docs/p1-mtu.md, wanbond-fixes.md). Design decision: daemon installs it for forwarded peers vs documented operator step.

Explore internal/sched/ (active_backup.go, weighted.go, scheduler.go), internal/config/config.go (pacing/BDP config, SchedulerPolicy), internal/bind/multipath.go (Send, Pick), internal/device/device.go (link setup), and the AGENTS.md testing/lint discipline (`just test`, `just lint` across default+e2e+realhosts tags; keep docs README/design/install in sync). Produce a fine-grained, correctly-sequenced, testable task-DAG. Each task needs a concrete `acceptance` (a command/observable/invariant, never "works"), a `suggestedModel` (frontier|standard|fast), a `milestone`, and `ledgerRefs` including "goals:G14" and (for fix tasks) "defects:D65". Include a task to update docs in sync with code, and tasks for unit tests of the pacing behavior. Emit the candidate JSON per the contract.

1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
10	)
11	
12	// Config tunes the active-backup scheduler.
13	type Config struct {
14		// FailbackAfter is the failback hysteresis dwell: once a higher-priority
15		// path recovers (returns to StateUp) while egress is on a lower-priority
16		// backup, the scheduler holds on the backup until the higher-priority path
17		// has stayed continuously Up for at least this long before failing egress
18		// BACK to it. This is the anti-thrash guard: a primary that flaps up/down
19		// never repeatedly steals egress back, because each dip restarts the dwell.
20		//
21		// Failover in the OTHER direction (active path going Down) is never delayed:
22		// the scheduler switches to the best remaining path immediately, since a dead
23		// active path cannot carry traffic while a timer runs. The asymmetry — instant
24		// failover, debounced failback — is what makes recovery non-thrashing.
25		FailbackAfter time.Duration
26	}
27	
28	// ActiveBackup is the P1 send-side scheduler: a single ACTIVE path (the
29	// highest-priority eligible one — index 0, the preferred primary, when it is
30	// up) carries ALL egress; every other path stays idle (data-thrift by
31	// construction). It reads per-path liveness from the injected T13 PathHealth
32	// sources and a clock for the failback dwell, so it is deterministic under test.
33	//
34	// Priority is the health-slice order: index 0 is the preferred primary, higher
35	// indices are progressively-lower-priority backups. A path is eligible when its
36	// PathHealth reports StateUp.
37	//
38	// Concurrency: the send path is concurrent, so Pick recomputes the active path
39	// under s.mu and every field it touches is guarded by that mutex. It never calls
40	// back into the Bind, so a Bind holding its own send lock across Pick cannot
41	// deadlock against it.
42	type ActiveBackup struct {
43		health []PathHealth // index = priority; [0] is the preferred primary
44		clock  telemetry.Clock
45		cfg    Config
46		log    log.Logger
47	
48		mu           sync.Mutex
49		active       int       // cached selection; -1 == no eligible path
50		pending      int       // failback candidate index; -1 == none pending
51		pendingSince time.Time // when the current failback candidate first became eligible
52	}
53	
54	// compile-time proof ActiveBackup is a (dynamic) Scheduler.
55	var (
56		_ Scheduler        = (*ActiveBackup)(nil)
57		_ DynamicScheduler = (*ActiveBackup)(nil)
58	)
59	
60	// NewActiveBackup builds the active-backup scheduler over health (priority
61	// order, index 0 = preferred primary). It fails fast on an empty health set or a
62	// nil dependency: the scheduler is a required, fully-wired collaborator, not an
63	// optional one.
64	func NewActiveBackup(health []PathHealth, cfg Config, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*ActiveBackup, error) {
65		if len(health) == 0 {
66			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
67		}
68		for i, h := range health {
69			if h == nil {
70				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
71			}
72		}
73		if clock == nil {
74			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
75		}
76		if logger == nil {
77			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
78		}
79		return &ActiveBackup{
80			health:  health,
81			clock:   clock,
82			cfg:     cfg,
83			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
84			active:  -1,
85			pending: -1,
86		}, nil
87	}
88	
89	// AddPath admits h as a new lowest-priority path and returns its index (the new
90	// tail). Appending at the tail leaves every existing index — including the cached
91	// active/pending — unchanged, and the new path can only become active once every
92	// higher-priority path is down, so a runtime admission never disturbs the active
93	// selection of the surviving paths (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers.
94	func (s *ActiveBackup) AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error) {
95		if h == nil {
96			return 0, fmt.Errorf("sched: cannot add a nil path health source")
97		}
98		s.mu.Lock()
99		defer s.mu.Unlock()
100		s.health = append(s.health, h)
101		return len(s.health) - 1, nil
102	}
103	
104	// RemovePath drops the health source at index i and repairs the cached selection
105	// so the surviving paths are undisturbed (T30). The active/pending indices are
106	// remapped by IDENTITY rather than by arithmetic: the survivor a cached index
107	// pointed at keeps being pointed at after the slice compaction, and a cached
108	// index that pointed at the removed path is cleared (-1). Clearing active makes
109	// the next Pick fail egress over to the best remaining path; clearing pending
110	// abandons an in-flight failback dwell whose candidate no longer exists. It is
111	// safe for concurrent callers.
112	func (s *ActiveBackup) RemovePath(i int) error {
113		s.mu.Lock()
114		defer s.mu.Unlock()
115		if i < 0 || i >= len(s.health) {
116			return fmt.Errorf("sched: remove path index %d out of range [0,%d)", i, len(s.health))
117		}
118		// Capture the identities the cached indices point at BEFORE compaction, unless
119		// they point at the path being removed (then they are cleared).
120		var activeH, pendingH PathHealth
121		if s.active >= 0 && s.active != i {
122			activeH = s.health[s.active]
123		}
124		if s.pending >= 0 && s.pending != i {
125			pendingH = s.health[s.pending]
126		}
127		s.health = append(s.health[:i], s.health[i+1:]...)
128		s.active = indexOfHealth(s.health, activeH)
129		s.pending = indexOfHealth(s.health, pendingH)
130		if s.pending < 0 {
131			s.pendingSince = time.Time{}
132		}
133		return nil
134	}
135	
136	// SetPaths replaces the entire health list with a defensive copy of health and
137	// clears the cached selection (active/pending), so the next Pick re-derives the
138	// active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on every Open to re-align the
139	// scheduler's membership with the freshly-rebuilt path slice, which is what makes a
140	// runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned (T30). It fails
141	// fast on an empty set or a nil element, matching NewActiveBackup's contract. It is
142	// safe for concurrent callers.
143	func (s *ActiveBackup) SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error {
144		if len(health) == 0 {
145			return fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
146		}
147		for i, h := range health {
148			if h == nil {
149				return fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
150			}
151		}
152		s.mu.Lock()
153		defer s.mu.Unlock()
154		s.health = append([]PathHealth(nil), health...)
155		s.active = -1
156		s.pending = -1
157		s.pendingSince = time.Time{}
158		return nil
159	}
160	
161	// indexOfHealth returns the index of h in hs by identity, or -1 when h is nil or
162	// absent. Interface equality is pointer identity for the concrete *Prober /
163	// health values the scheduler holds.
164	func indexOfHealth(hs []PathHealth, h PathHealth) int {
165		if h == nil {
166			return -1
167		}
168		for i := range hs {
169			if hs[i] == h {
170				return i
171			}
172		}
173		return -1
174	}
175	
176	// Pick returns the index of the current active path, recomputing the selection
177	// against live path health and the clock. It is safe for concurrent callers.
178	// class is ignored: active-backup has no pacer, so every frame class is treated
179	// identically (defect D22's control-frame exemption is a pacing concern).
180	func (s *ActiveBackup) Pick(_ FrameClass) int {
181		s.mu.Lock()
182		defer s.mu.Unlock()
183		s.recomputeLocked(s.clock.Now())
184		return s.active
185	}
186	
187	// Recompute re-derives the active path from current liveness and the failback
188	// dwell, exactly as Pick does, but discards the result. Active-backup's selection
189	// is a single cached index recomputed purely from liveness and the clock, so Pick
190	// is idempotent and Recompute IS Pick-without-the-return: the eager-failover nudge
191	// (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute through it with no
192	// behavioural difference from the old Pick-based nudge. It is safe for concurrent
193	// callers.
194	func (s *ActiveBackup) Recompute() {
195		s.mu.Lock()
196		defer s.mu.Unlock()
197		s.recomputeLocked(s.clock.Now())
198	}
199	
200	// recomputeLocked re-derives the active path from current liveness and the
201	// failback dwell. Caller holds s.mu.
202	//
203	// Selection rule, in priority order:
204	//   - best = the lowest-index (highest-priority) path currently StateUp.
205	//   - No path up -> no active path.
206	//   - Cold start, or the current active is no longer up (best is a
207	//     lower-priority path) -> switch immediately (instant failover).
208	//   - best already IS the active -> stable, nothing to do.
209	//   - best is a HIGHER-priority path than the active (the primary recovered)
210	//     -> a failback candidate: hold on the current active until best has been
211	//     continuously up for FailbackAfter, THEN fail back. If the current active
212	//     itself goes down mid-dwell, move to best at once rather than stall.
213	func (s *ActiveBackup) recomputeLocked(now time.Time) {
214		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
215	
216		if best < 0 {
217			s.pending = -1
218			s.setActiveLocked(-1, "no eligible path")
219			return
220		}
221		if s.active < 0 || best > s.active {
222			// Cold start, or the active path is no longer eligible (best, the first
223			// eligible index, sits below it) -> immediate (fail)over.
224			s.pending = -1
225			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover")
226			return
227		}
228		if best == s.active {
229			s.pending = -1
230			return
231		}
232	
233		// best < s.active: a higher-priority path recovered -> debounced failback.
234		if s.pending != best {
235			s.pending = best
236			s.pendingSince = now
237		}
238		if now.Sub(s.pendingSince) >= s.cfg.FailbackAfter {
239			s.pending = -1
240			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failback")
241			return
242		}
243		// Still within the dwell: keep the current active while it stays eligible; if
244		// it has gone down, we cannot stall on it — move to best now.
245		if s.health[s.active].State() != telemetry.StateUp {
246			s.pending = -1
247			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover during failback dwell")
248		}
249	}
250	
251	// bestEligibleLocked returns the lowest-index path reporting StateUp, or -1.
252	func (s *ActiveBackup) bestEligibleLocked() int {
253		for i := range s.health {
254			if s.health[i].State() == telemetry.StateUp {
255				return i
256			}
257		}
258		return -1
259	}
260	
261	// setActiveLocked stores the active index and logs a transition only when it
262	// actually changes, so a saturated send path does not log on every Pick.
263	func (s *ActiveBackup) setActiveLocked(idx int, reason string) {
264		if s.active == idx {
265			return
266		}
267		from := s.active
268		s.active = idx
269		s.log.Info("scheduler active path change",
270			"from", from,
271			"to", idx,
272			"reason", reason,
273		)
274	}

1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates
93	// with no real probe stream.
94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through
144	// the Prober") and its State() is an unguarded field read (liveness.go), so
145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a
155	// follow-up task) drives real per-path *telemetry.Prober liveness: with every
156	// path statically Up, the active-backup scheduler keeps all egress on the
157	// preferred primary (the data-thrift bring-up behaviour), and swapping in real
158	// Probers later activates failover with no further Bind or scheduler change.
159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
160	
161	// State reports the path as up.
162	func (AlwaysUp) State() telemetry.PathState { return telemetry.StateUp }

1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"math"
6		"sync"
7		"time"
8	
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
11	)
12	
13	// WeightedConfig tunes the weighted-aggregation scheduler. The device builds it
14	// from config.SchedulerConfig; sched holds its own struct so the package does not
15	// depend on config. Every field is validated by NewWeighted (fail-fast at the
16	// composition root), mirroring the config-boundary validation.
17	type WeightedConfig struct {
18		// PerPathCapacity is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots per
19		// second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against,
20		// and (when Pacing is on) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. There is no
21		// measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity is expressed in the only unit the scheduler
22		// observes — Pick() invocations per second. Must be > 0.
23		PerPathCapacity float64
24		// EngageFraction engages aggregation once smoothed offered load exceeds
25		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacity. In (0,1].
26		EngageFraction float64
27		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once smoothed offered
28		// load stays below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacity for CollapseDwell. In
29		// [0,EngageFraction): the gap is the hysteresis band that keeps the metered path
30		// from dribbling on/off around a single threshold (data-thrift, requirement 2).
31		DisengageFraction float64
32		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
33		// (the temporal half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell).
34		CollapseDwell time.Duration
35		// LoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load rate
36		// estimator. Must be > 0.
37		LoadTau time.Duration
38	
39		// Pacing turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are bypassed
40		// (a documented no-op: P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the unmetered
41		// fixture), but PerPathCapacity still drives the aggregation gate.
42		Pacing bool
43		// PacingBurst is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0 when
44		// Pacing is on.
45		PacingBurst float64
46	
47		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula so a cold path (no RTT samples
48		// yet, near-zero RTT) cannot be handed unbounded weight. Must be > 0.
49		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration
50		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss path
51		// gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
52		// alone. Must be > 0.
53		WeightLossFloor float64
54	}
55	
56	func (c WeightedConfig) validate() error {
57		if c.PerPathCapacity <= 0 {
58			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted PerPathCapacity must be > 0, got %g", c.PerPathCapacity)
59		}
60		if c.EngageFraction <= 0 || c.EngageFraction > 1 {
61			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted EngageFraction must be in (0,1], got %g", c.EngageFraction)
62		}
63		if c.DisengageFraction < 0 || c.DisengageFraction >= c.EngageFraction {
64			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted DisengageFraction must be in [0,EngageFraction=%g), got %g", c.EngageFraction, c.DisengageFraction)
65		}
66		if c.CollapseDwell < 0 {
67			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted CollapseDwell must be >= 0, got %s", c.CollapseDwell)
68		}
69		if c.LoadTau <= 0 {
70			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted LoadTau must be > 0, got %s", c.LoadTau)
71		}
72		if c.Pacing && c.PacingBurst <= 0 {
73			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted PacingBurst must be > 0 when pacing is enabled, got %g", c.PacingBurst)
74		}
75		if c.WeightRTTFloor <= 0 {
76			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted WeightRTTFloor must be > 0, got %s", c.WeightRTTFloor)
77		}
78		if c.WeightLossFloor <= 0 {
79			return fmt.Errorf("sched: weighted WeightLossFloor must be > 0, got %g", c.WeightLossFloor)
80		}
81		return nil
82	}
83	
84	// FECPolicy is the P3+ forward-error-correction extension seam. Given the path a
85	// frame was scheduled onto and the current eligible set, it returns ADDITIONAL path
86	// indices the frame should ALSO egress on for redundancy (e.g. a repair copy on a
87	// second path). This is a documented hook, NOT an implementation: the current
88	// single-path Scheduler.Pick returns one index, so wiring redundant transmission
89	// requires the P3 Bind change that fans a frame out to several sockets. Until then
90	// a nil policy adds no redundancy and RedundantPaths returns nothing, so the seam is
91	// inert but present and unit-tested.
92	type FECPolicy interface {
93		RedundantPaths(chosen int, eligible []int) []int
94	}
95	
96	// WeightedScheduler is the T21 send-side scheduler: a weighted-aggregation policy
97	// that, under load, stripes a single flow across every eligible path in proportion
98	// to a per-path RTT/loss-derived weight, and at low load COLLAPSES to the primary
99	// (index 0) so the metered backup path stays ~idle (data-thrift, requirement 2). It
100	// engages the backup only once offered load exceeds one path's capacity and
101	// disengages with hysteresis (a two-threshold band plus a collapse dwell) so the
102	// metered path never dribbles on/off. Per-path send-pacing (token buckets) bounds
103	// each path's egress rate so aggregation does not build a standing queue that
104	// inflates latency-under-load (P0 §7); pacing is a config no-op when disabled.
105	//
106	// It reads per-path liveness from PathHealth (the T13 up/down verdict) and per-path
107	// quality from PathQuality (the T13 Estimate: RTT/loss). In production BOTH are the
108	// SAME *telemetry.Prober per path; the unit tests drive synthetic sources.
109	//
110	// Weight formula (Mathis-style throughput proxy). A single steady-state TCP flow's
111	// bandwidth is ∝ 1/(RTT·√p) (Mathis et al.); used here as a RELATIVE path-quality
112	// proxy it directs more of the aggregate flow onto the path a flow would run faster
113	// on. Per eligible path:
114	//
115	//	w_i = 1 / ( max(RTT_i, RTTFloor) · sqrt(Loss_i + LossFloor) )
116	//
117	// normalized over the eligible set. RTTFloor bounds a cold/zero-RTT path; LossFloor
118	// keeps a zero-loss path finite (two zero-loss paths then split by inverse-RTT
119	// alone). There is no measured bandwidth to use (Estimate is RTT/Jitter/Loss only,
120	// P0 §7), so an RTT/loss proxy is the honest choice.
121	//
122	// Concurrency mirrors ActiveBackup: every field is guarded by s.mu; the send path
123	// recomputes under the lock and never calls back into the Bind, so a Bind holding
124	// its own send lock across Pick cannot deadlock.
125	//
126	// Statefulness and the T40 nudge. Pick is STATEFUL — it advances the offered-load
127	// meter, the aggregation gate, the pacing buckets, and the smooth-weighted-round-
128	// robin credits, so ONE Pick == ONE frame. The eager-failover nudge (defect D18)
129	// must therefore NOT call Pick (it would steal a distribution slot and skew the
130	// weights); it calls Recompute, which refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/
131	// active set and logs any active-path transition, touching NONE of that per-frame
132	// distribution state.
133	type WeightedScheduler struct {
134		health  []PathHealth  // index = priority; [0] is the preferred primary
135		quality []PathQuality // parallel to health; element may be nil (neutral weight)
136		clock   telemetry.Clock
137		cfg     WeightedConfig
138		log     log.Logger
139		fec     FECPolicy // P3+ redundancy seam; nil = no FEC
140	
141		mu sync.Mutex
142	
143		// Offered-load meter: an exponentially-weighted event-rate estimator (each Pick is
144		// one event; the estimate converges to the offered Pick rate in events/sec).
145		loadRate float64
146		haveLoad bool
147		lastLoad time.Time
148	
149		// Aggregation gate (hysteresis).
150		aggregating bool
151		belowSince  time.Time // when load first fell below the disengage threshold; zero == not pending
152	
153		// Smooth-weighted-round-robin credits, parallel to health (global path index).
154		current []float64
155	
156		// Per-path pacing token buckets, parallel to health.
157		tokens   []float64
158		lastFill time.Time
159		haveFill bool
160	
161		// Active-primary cache: the best eligible path, for eager-failover transition
162		// logging (parity with ActiveBackup). -1 == none.
163		active int
164	
165		// Pacer-shedding log rate limiter: under sustained overload shedding is per-frame
166		// (thousands/s), so the "pacer shedding" diagnostic is coalesced to at most one
167		// record per shedLogInterval, carrying the count shed since the last record.
168		shedCount   int
169		lastShedLog time.Time
170	}
171	
172	// shedLogInterval bounds how often the pacer-shedding diagnostic is emitted, so a
173	// sustained overload logs a periodic coalesced record rather than one line per
174	// dropped frame.
175	const shedLogInterval = 1 * time.Second
176	
177	// compile-time proof WeightedScheduler is a (dynamic) Scheduler.
178	var (
179		_ Scheduler        = (*WeightedScheduler)(nil)
180		_ DynamicScheduler = (*WeightedScheduler)(nil)
181	)
182	
183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a
186	// neutral weight until a real PathQuality is wired). It fails fast on an empty or
187	// length-mismatched set, a nil health element, a nil dependency, or an invalid config.
188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))
194		}
195		for i, h := range health {
196			if h == nil {
197				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
198			}
199		}
200		if clock == nil {
201			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
202		}
203		if logger == nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
205		}
206		if err := cfg.validate(); err != nil {
207			return nil, err
208		}
209		return &WeightedScheduler{
210			health:  append([]PathHealth(nil), health...),
211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
215			current: make([]float64, len(health)),
216			tokens:  fullBuckets(len(health), cfg.PacingBurst),
217			active:  -1,
218		}, nil
219	}
220	
221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}
230	
231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {
240			return nil
241		}
242		return s.fec.RedundantPaths(chosen, s.eligibleLocked())
243	}
244	
245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}
280	
281	// loadGapLocked returns the wall-clock gap since the previous offered-load sample, or
282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.
283	func (s *WeightedScheduler) loadGapLocked(now time.Time) time.Duration {
284		if !s.haveLoad {
285			return 0
286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {
289			gap = 0
290		}
291		return gap
292	}
293	
294	// Recompute refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/active set from the current
295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame
296	// distribution state (load meter, gate, pacing, round-robin credits), so the eager-
297	// failover nudge (defect D18/T40) drives an egress-lull failover recompute through it
298	// WITHOUT stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. It is
299	// safe for concurrent callers.
300	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Recompute() {
301		now := s.clock.Now()
302		s.mu.Lock()
303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)
305	}
306	
307	// recomputeLocked refreshes the active-primary cache (best eligible path) and logs a
308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.
309	func (s *WeightedScheduler) recomputeLocked(_ time.Time) {
310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
311		s.setActiveLocked(best)
312	}
313	
314	// serveLocked applies pacing to a single chosen path: it returns idx when the frame
315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
329	
330	// selectAggregatingLocked picks a path proportional to weight (smooth weighted round
331	// robin) and applies pacing. When the round-robin's ideal pick is paced out it falls
332	// through to any other eligible path that still has a token, so aggregate egress is
333	// bounded by the SUM of the per-path paces; when every eligible path is paced out it
334	// sheds the frame (PickPaced). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it
335	// egresses on the weight-ideal path spending no token and is never shed, so bulk
336	// overload across every path cannot starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
337	func (s *WeightedScheduler) selectAggregatingLocked(now time.Time, eligible []int, weights []float64, class FrameClass) int {
338		ideal := s.swrrPickLocked(eligible, weights)
339		if class == ClassControl {
340			return ideal
341		}
342		if s.tryConsumeLocked(ideal) {
343			return ideal
344		}
345		for _, gi := range eligible {
346			if gi == ideal {
347				continue
348			}
349			if s.tryConsumeLocked(gi) {
350				return gi
351			}
352		}
353		return s.shedLocked(now)
354	}
355	
356	// shedLocked records one paced-out (shed) frame and emits the coalesced "pacer
357	// shedding" diagnostic at most once per shedLogInterval. It returns PickPaced so the
358	// Send path can map the drop to a distinct, non-outage error/log (criticism #3): the
359	// paths are healthy, this is deliberate rate limiting. Caller holds s.mu.
360	func (s *WeightedScheduler) shedLocked(now time.Time) int {
361		s.shedCount++
362		if s.lastShedLog.IsZero() || now.Sub(s.lastShedLog) >= shedLogInterval {
363			s.log.Info("scheduler pacer shedding",
364				"shed_frames", s.shedCount,
365				"load_fps", s.loadRate,
366			)
367			s.lastShedLog = now
368			s.shedCount = 0
369		}
370		return PickPaced
371	}
372	
373	// swrrPickLocked is nginx's smooth weighted round-robin over the eligible set: it
374	// advances each eligible path's credit by its weight, picks the max-credit path, and
375	// subtracts the total weight from the winner. Over many calls the selection frequency
376	// of each path converges to its weight share, interleaved smoothly rather than
377	// bursted. Caller holds s.mu; weights is aligned to eligible order.
378	func (s *WeightedScheduler) swrrPickLocked(eligible []int, weights []float64) int {
379		var total float64
380		for _, w := range weights {
381			total += w
382		}
383		best := -1
384		var bestCur float64
385		for k, gi := range eligible {
386			s.current[gi] += weights[k]
387			if best < 0 || s.current[gi] > bestCur {
388				best = gi
389				bestCur = s.current[gi]
390			}
391		}
392		s.current[best] -= total
393		return best
394	}
395	
396	// weightsLocked computes the normalized Mathis-style weight for each eligible path
397	// from its PathQuality Estimate (RTT/loss). A path with NO usable quality — a nil
398	// quality source, or an all-zero Estimate (RTT==0: no probe sample yet) — is treated
399	// as genuinely NEUTRAL: it receives the MEAN of the measured paths' weights, so an
400	// unwired/cold path splits evenly rather than dominating. This matters because the
401	// floors would otherwise hand an all-zero Estimate the MAXIMUM weight (RTT floored to
402	// WeightRTTFloor, zero loss), letting a health-only path admitted via AddPath siphon
403	// the dominant share — the opposite of neutral. When every eligible path is neutral,
404	// all get equal weight. Caller holds s.mu; the result is aligned to eligible order and
405	// sums to 1.
406	func (s *WeightedScheduler) weightsLocked(eligible []int) []float64 {
407		w := make([]float64, len(eligible))
408		measured := make([]bool, len(eligible))
409		rttFloor := float64(s.cfg.WeightRTTFloor)
410		var sumMeasured float64
411		nMeasured := 0
412		for k, gi := range eligible {
413			q := s.quality[gi]
414			if q == nil {
415				continue // unwired: neutral, filled below
416			}
417			est := q.Estimate()
418			if est.RTT <= 0 {
419				continue // no RTT sample yet (cold estimator): neutral, filled below
420			}
421			rtt := float64(est.RTT)
422			if rtt < rttFloor {
423				rtt = rttFloor
424			}
425			loss := est.Loss
426			if loss < 0 {
427				loss = 0
428			}
429			wi := 1.0 / (rtt * math.Sqrt(loss+s.cfg.WeightLossFloor))
430			w[k] = wi
431			measured[k] = true
432			sumMeasured += wi
433			nMeasured++
434		}
435		// Neutral weight: the mean of the measured weights (so a neutral path splits evenly
436		// with the measured set, never outweighing it); equal weights when nothing measured.
437		neutral := 1.0
438		if nMeasured > 0 {
439			neutral = sumMeasured / float64(nMeasured)
440		}
441		var sum float64
442		for k := range w {
443			if !measured[k] {
444				w[k] = neutral
445			}
446			sum += w[k]
447		}
448		if sum <= 0 {
449			for k := range w {
450				w[k] = 1.0 / float64(len(w))
451			}
452			return w
453		}
454		for k := range w {
455			w[k] /= sum
456		}
457		return w
458	}
459	
460	// observeLoadLocked folds one offered frame into the exponentially-weighted event-
461	// rate estimator. Each event adds 1/tau (per-second units) after decaying the prior
462	// estimate by exp(-dt/tau); the steady-state estimate converges to the offered rate
463	// in events/sec. It is robust to dt==0 (many Picks at one clock instant just add
464	// without decay). Caller holds s.mu.
465	func (s *WeightedScheduler) observeLoadLocked(now time.Time) {
466		tau := s.cfg.LoadTau.Seconds()
467		if !s.haveLoad {
468			s.haveLoad = true
469			s.lastLoad = now
470			s.loadRate = 0
471		} else {
472			dt := now.Sub(s.lastLoad).Seconds()
473			if dt < 0 {
474				dt = 0
475			}
476			s.loadRate *= math.Exp(-dt / tau)
477			s.lastLoad = now
478		}
479		s.loadRate += 1.0 / tau
480	}
481	
482	// updateGateLocked engages or disengages aggregation from the smoothed offered load,
483	// with hysteresis: engage the instant load exceeds EngageFraction*capacity; collapse
484	// back to primary-only only after load stays below DisengageFraction*capacity for
485	// CollapseDwell. The two-threshold band plus the dwell keep the metered path from
486	// flapping on/off around a single point (requirement 2). gap is the wall-clock idle
487	// span since the previous offered frame.
488	//
489	// Idle time IS low load (criticism #1). The gate only advances on a Pick, so an
490	// overload that stops ABRUPTLY (load still above disengage on the last frame) would
491	// otherwise stay engaged across an arbitrarily long idle span and stripe the first
492	// ~CollapseDwell of the NEXT low burst onto the metered backup — a data-thrift leak.
493	// Two provisions close it: (a) an idle gap that alone reaches CollapseDwell forces an
494	// immediate collapse, since a gap that long is itself proof of sustained low load; and
495	// (b) belowSince is BACKDATED to when load actually dropped (the previous sample,
496	// now-gap) rather than seeded at this post-idle frame, so the dwell counts the idle.
497	// The load EWMA decays across gap in observeLoadLocked, so a post-idle frame reads
498	// ~0 and the collapse fires on the FIRST frame of the next burst. Caller holds s.mu.
499	func (s *WeightedScheduler) updateGateLocked(now time.Time, gap time.Duration) {
500		engage := s.cfg.EngageFraction * s.cfg.PerPathCapacity
501		disengage := s.cfg.DisengageFraction * s.cfg.PerPathCapacity
502		if !s.aggregating {
503			if s.loadRate > engage {
504				s.aggregating = true
505				s.belowSince = time.Time{}
506				s.log.Info("scheduler aggregation change", "to", "aggregating", "load_fps", s.loadRate)
507			}
508			return
509		}
510		// (a) A single idle gap of at least the dwell is conclusive: collapse now.
511		if gap >= s.cfg.CollapseDwell {
512			s.aggregating = false
513			s.belowSince = time.Time{}
514			s.log.Info("scheduler aggregation change", "to", "collapsed", "reason", "idle gap", "gap", gap.String())
515			return
516		}
517		if s.loadRate < disengage {
518			if s.belowSince.IsZero() {
519				// (b) Backdate to when load dropped: the previous sample (now-gap), not this
520				// post-idle frame, so the idle span counts toward the dwell.
521				s.belowSince = now.Add(-gap)
522			}
523			if now.Sub(s.belowSince) >= s.cfg.CollapseDwell {
524				s.aggregating = false
525				s.belowSince = time.Time{}
526				s.log.Info("scheduler aggregation change", "to", "collapsed", "reason", "sustained low load", "load_fps", s.loadRate)
527			}
528			return
529		}
530		// Load rose back above the disengage threshold: reset the collapse dwell.
531		s.belowSince = time.Time{}
532	}
533	
534	// refillLocked tops up every per-path token bucket by PerPathCapacity·elapsed, capped
535	// at PacingBurst. The first call seeds every bucket full. It is a no-op accountant
536	// when pacing is disabled (tryConsumeLocked always admits), but the buckets are still
537	// maintained so toggling pacing needs no re-seed. Caller holds s.mu.
538	func (s *WeightedScheduler) refillLocked(now time.Time) {
539		if !s.haveFill {
540			s.haveFill = true
541			s.lastFill = now
542			for i := range s.tokens {
543				s.tokens[i] = s.cfg.PacingBurst
544			}
545			return
546		}
547		dt := now.Sub(s.lastFill).Seconds()
548		if dt < 0 {
549			dt = 0
550		}
551		add := s.cfg.PerPathCapacity * dt
552		for i := range s.tokens {
553			s.tokens[i] += add
554			if s.tokens[i] > s.cfg.PacingBurst {
555				s.tokens[i] = s.cfg.PacingBurst
556			}
557		}
558		s.lastFill = now
559	}
560	
561	// tryConsumeLocked spends one token from path idx's bucket, reporting whether the
562	// frame is admitted. With pacing disabled it always admits (the buckets are inert).
563	// Caller holds s.mu.
564	func (s *WeightedScheduler) tryConsumeLocked(idx int) bool {
565		if !s.cfg.Pacing {
566			return true
567		}
568		if s.tokens[idx] >= 1 {
569			s.tokens[idx]--
570			return true
571		}
572		return false
573	}
574	
575	// eligibleLocked returns the global indices of paths reporting StateUp, in priority
576	// order. Caller holds s.mu.
577	func (s *WeightedScheduler) eligibleLocked() []int {
578		e := make([]int, 0, len(s.health))
579		for i := range s.health {
580			if s.health[i].State() == telemetry.StateUp {
581				e = append(e, i)
582			}
583		}
584		return e
585	}
586	
587	// bestEligibleLocked returns the lowest-index path reporting StateUp, or -1.
588	func (s *WeightedScheduler) bestEligibleLocked() int {
589		for i := range s.health {
590			if s.health[i].State() == telemetry.StateUp {
591				return i
592			}
593		}
594		return -1
595	}
596	
597	// setActiveLocked stores the active-primary index and logs a transition only when it
598	// changes, so a saturated send path does not log on every Pick. The message matches
599	// ActiveBackup's ("scheduler active path change") so downstream log parsing (the e2e
600	// failover harness) reads either policy identically.
601	func (s *WeightedScheduler) setActiveLocked(idx int) {
602		if s.active == idx {
603			return
604		}
605		from := s.active
606		s.active = idx
607		s.log.Info("scheduler active path change", "from", from, "to", idx, "reason", "weighted recompute")
608	}
609	
610	// AddPath admits h as a new lowest-priority path and returns its index (the new
611	// tail). Its quality source is h itself when h also satisfies PathQuality (the
612	// production *Prober does); a health-ONLY source (no PathQuality) is recorded with a
613	// nil quality and weightsLocked then gives it the NEUTRAL (mean) weight, so it splits
614	// evenly and never siphons the dominant share. Appending at the tail leaves every
615	// existing index unchanged and the new path only carries traffic once aggregation
616	// engages, so a runtime admission never disturbs the surviving paths' distribution
617	// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers.
618	func (s *WeightedScheduler) AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error) {
619		if h == nil {
620			return 0, fmt.Errorf("sched: cannot add a nil path health source")
621		}
622		s.mu.Lock()
623		defer s.mu.Unlock()
624		s.health = append(s.health, h)
625		s.quality = append(s.quality, asQuality(h))
626		s.current = append(s.current, 0)
627		s.tokens = append(s.tokens, s.cfg.PacingBurst)
628		return len(s.health) - 1, nil
629	}
630	
631	// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one) and
632	// clears the cached active selection so the next Pick/Recompute re-derives it from
633	// live liveness. The round-robin credits are positional and compacted with the path
634	// list; a brief re-warm of the smoothing after a membership change is harmless. It is
635	// safe for concurrent callers.
636	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RemovePath(i int) error {
637		s.mu.Lock()
638		defer s.mu.Unlock()
639		if i < 0 || i >= len(s.health) {
640			return fmt.Errorf("sched: remove path index %d out of range [0,%d)", i, len(s.health))
641		}
642		s.health = append(s.health[:i], s.health[i+1:]...)
643		s.quality = append(s.quality[:i], s.quality[i+1:]...)
644		s.current = append(s.current[:i], s.current[i+1:]...)
645		s.tokens = append(s.tokens[:i], s.tokens[i+1:]...)
646		s.active = -1
647		return nil
648	}
649	
650	// SetPaths replaces the entire health list (priority order, index 0 = preferred
651	// primary), rebuilding the parallel quality slice from the new sources, resetting the
652	// round-robin credits and pacing buckets, and collapsing aggregation to primary-only
653	// so the next Pick re-derives everything from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
654	// every Open to re-align the scheduler with the freshly-rebuilt path slice (T30). It
655	// fails fast on an empty set or a nil element, matching NewWeighted. It is safe for
656	// concurrent callers.
657	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error {
658		if len(health) == 0 {
659			return fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
660		}
661		for i, h := range health {
662			if h == nil {
663				return fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
664			}
665		}
666		s.mu.Lock()
667		defer s.mu.Unlock()
668		s.health = append([]PathHealth(nil), health...)
669		s.quality = make([]PathQuality, len(health))
670		for i, h := range health {
671			s.quality[i] = asQuality(h)
672		}
673		s.current = make([]float64, len(health))
674		s.tokens = fullBuckets(len(health), s.cfg.PacingBurst)
675		s.haveFill = false
676		s.aggregating = false
677		s.belowSince = time.Time{}
678		s.active = -1
679		return nil
680	}
681	
682	// asQuality returns h as a PathQuality when it also implements that interface (the
683	// production *telemetry.Prober does — one source drives both liveness and weight), or
684	// nil so the path gets a neutral weight.
685	func asQuality(h PathHealth) PathQuality {
686		if q, ok := h.(PathQuality); ok {
687			return q
688		}
689		return nil
690	}
691	
692	// fullBuckets returns n token buckets each seeded to burst.
693	func fullBuckets(n int, burst float64) []float64 {
694		t := make([]float64, n)
695		for i := range t {
696			t[i] = burst
697		}
698		return t
699	}

{"item":{"id":"G14","milestoneId":"M56","status":"planning","fields":{"title":"Fix D65: shape egress on the default path + clamp forwarded-TCP MSS","description":"DEFECT-SEEDED goal (from confirmed root cause of D65 — skip clarification, proceed straight to planning).

CONFIRMED ROOT CAUSE: single-flow TCP collapses to ~3.67 Mbps because the loss + ~1s bufferbloat originate in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer, which wanbond's DEFAULT active-backup scheduler does nothing to shape (egress pacing/AQM is a weighted-only feature: internal/config/config.go:99-108, 218-223). The WAN carries ≥6.9 Mbps (UDP got 6.9 vs TCP 3.67 on the same path); the tunnel's encode CPU has 40-80x headroom (~2429 Mbps/core x86, ~160-300 Mbps/core Pi4) and there is no wanbond-internal queue (synchronous send: multipath.go:2027-2035) — so the unshaped sender overruns the Starlink buffer (build-to-1s-then-13%-drop = overflow signature) and TCP cwnd collapses. Compounding: no TCP MSS clamp is installed anywhere for FORWARDED traffic (daemon installs none: internal/device/device.go:52-55; deploy recipes omit it: docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, install.md, wanbond-fixes.md).

SUGGESTED FIX (verbatim from D65):
(1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally a CoDel-style AQM drop) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery (internal/sched/weighted.go token bucket + internal/config SizePacingFromBDP / BurstFrames), so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate and cannot self-inflict the ~1s Starlink bufferbloat. Expected: eliminate the standing queue, restore single-flow TCP toward the ≥6.9 Mbps the WAN carries. Design decision: wire pacing into active-backup directly vs expose a policy-independent pacing knob.
(2) SECONDARY — install a TCP MSS clamp on wanbond0 for forwarded traffic (mangle FORWARD -o wanbond0 TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu) and document it in the deploy recipes (install.md §9.2, wanbond-fixes.md). Design decision: daemon installs it for forwarded peers vs documented operator step.

VALIDATION: on-hardware three-way iperf3 (direct-WAN / tunnel / loopback) + loaded-RTT A/B on the Pi4/Starlink/o3 is deferred to verify/implement (the user waived pre-fix field measurement per Q56). NON-GOAL (low priority, NOT the D65 ceiling): send-path micro-optimizations (dst-reuse seam, GSO/sendmmsg batching, SIMD chacha20).","sourceRefs":["defects:D65"],"grounding":"defect-seeded"},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:58:21.455Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:58:21.455Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}}

{"item":{"id":"D65","milestoneId":"M49","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"Tunnel single-flow throughput plateaus at ~3.67 Mbps with loss/reorder-limited TCP and ~1s bufferbloat under load","severity":"high","description":"Root-cause the tunnel's low single-flow throughput and its loss/bufferbloat under load.

**Observations (Pi 4 edge, Starlink active path ~40 ms RTT, o3 concentrator):**
- Single-stream **TCP** through the tunnel plateaus at **~3.67 Mbps**, with `cwnd` stuck at ~30 KB and **13 retransmits / 10 s** — i.e. loss/reordering-limited, not a clean rate cap.
- **UDP** offered at 8 Mbps delivers ~6.9 Mbps at **13% datagram loss** with the queue building to **~1 s loaded RTT** (idle ~40 ms).
- Plain WireGuard on aarch64 normally does far more than 3.67 Mbps, so the ceiling and the retransmit pattern warrant explanation.

**Investigate — isolate the ceiling's source and any fixable inefficiency:**
1. Separate the three candidate limiters with measurements: (a) direct iperf3 over the same WAN (no tunnel) = WAN ceiling; (b) tunnel over the WAN = end-to-end; (c) a loopback / netns tunnel (no WAN) = pure CPU/codec ceiling. Attribute the gap.
2. If CPU-bound: profile the send path — WG crypto vs reed-solomon FEC encode vs the bonding DATA-frame codec (`internal/bind/`) vs the userspace TUN read/write copy. Look for per-frame allocation, absence of send batching / GSO, single-goroutine serialization, or an XChaCha20/FEC hot loop.
3. If loss/reorder-bound: is the loss introduced by the tunnel (framing/FEC/scheduler) or by the WAN? Check MSS clamping on `wanbond0` (inner MTU 1400 → is TCP fragmenting/PMTU black-holing?), and whether active-backup ever briefly reorders at path selection.

Deliverable: a profile + the dominant cost, whether ~3.67 Mbps is the true CPU/WAN ceiling or an inefficiency, and a concrete fix candidate (batching, buffer reuse, MSS clamp) with the expected gain.","rootCause":"The single-flow throughput collapse is LOSS-INDUCED, not a CPU or raw-WAN rate cap, and the loss originates in the EXTERNAL Starlink last-mile buffer that wanbond does nothing to shape.

(1) The WAN demonstrably carries ≥6.9 Mbps: UDP offered at 8 Mbps delivered 6.9 through the same tunnel/path while single-flow TCP got only 3.67, so TCP's shortfall is loss-induced cwnd collapse (cwnd stuck ~30KB over ~40ms RTT), not a rate cap [D65 measurements].
(2) The tunnel's own processing is not the ceiling: measured DATA-codec encode = 4610 ns/op ≈ 2429 Mbps/core on x86_64 (pprof: chacha20-dominated, generic non-SIMD path), ~160-300 Mbps/core extrapolated to Pi4 aarch64 — 40-80x above 3.67 Mbps; and 3.67 Mbps ≈ 300 pps is trivially within the syscall/lock budget [internal/frame inline benchmark].
(3) There is no wanbond-internal queue: Send writes each frame synchronously to the UDP socket [internal/bind/multipath.go:2027-2035], the pacer sheds at the head and is BDP-bounded [internal/config/config.go:218-223], and the resequencer is bounded in memory+latency [internal/reseq/reseq.go:90-96] — so the observed ~1s loaded RTT is the EXTERNAL Starlink buffer, and its build-to-~1s-then-~13%-drop is the classic buffer-overflow signature of an unshaped sender (random medium loss would not grow the queue).
(4) Under the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler wanbond applies NO egress pacing/AQM (pacing is a weighted-only feature) [internal/config/config.go:99-108], so it offers packets to the bloated last-mile unshaped and cannot prevent the overflow. This is the primary controllable root cause.
(5) Compounding, for FORWARDED (behind-tunnel) TCP no MSS clamp is installed anywhere — the daemon installs none [internal/device/device.go:52-55] and the deploy recipes omit it even for the client-LAN forwarding case [docs/p1-mtu.md:76-99, docs/install.md, wanbond-fixes.md] — so forwarded segments can fragment/PMTUD-blackhole (Pi-originated TCP is already MSS-bounded by the TUN route MTU; only forwarded flows are affected).

Candidates H-B (scheduler reorder), H-C (CPU-bound encode), H-D (internal oversized queue), H-E (FEC overhead) were each ruled out with validated evidence (H5/H6/H7/H8 = wrong). The user reviewed this diagnosis (Q56) and authorized implementing the fixes directly, waiving prior field-measurement confirmation; exact quantified gain is deferred to on-hardware validation.","suggestedFix":"Two independent fixes (user authorized direct implementation via Q56):
(1) PRIMARY — add BDP-sized egress send-pacing (optionally a CoDel-style AQM drop) to the DEFAULT active-backup scheduler, reusing the existing weighted-scheduler pacer + BDP-sizing machinery (internal/sched/weighted.go token bucket + internal/config SizePacingFromBDP / BurstFrames), so a single uplink is shaped to its drain rate and cannot self-inflict the ~1s Starlink bufferbloat. Expected: eliminate the standing queue and restore single-flow TCP toward the ≥6.9 Mbps the WAN demonstrably carries. (Design choice for plan-flow: wire pacing into active-backup directly vs expose a pacing knob usable independent of policy=weighted.)
(2) SECONDARY — install a TCP MSS clamp on wanbond0 for forwarded traffic (iptables/ip6tables mangle FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu) and document it in the deploy recipes (docs/install.md §9.2, wanbond-fixes.md), so behind-tunnel TCP cannot emit oversize segments that fragment/PMTUD-blackhole. Decide in plan-flow whether the daemon should install this itself for peers it forwards vs document it as an operator step.

Separately worth noting (NOT the D65 ceiling, low priority): the send path has un-taken optimizations — unused dst-reuse seam in frame.Codec.Encode, no GSO/sendmmsg batching (multipathBatchSize=1), generic (non-SIMD) chacha20 — which matter only at far higher rates.

On-hardware validation (three-way iperf3 + loaded-RTT A/B on Pi4/Starlink/o3) is deferred to verify/implement.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-122159-ac772045578314808.md",".cq/logs/20260714-122159-aa7a4cc064596f222.md",".cq/logs/20260714-122159-ae52507d7fc1a55b7.md",".cq/logs/20260714-122159-a7875c1b02b7ec340.md",".cq/logs/20260714-122159-a971f55e45232ce3d.md",".cq/logs/20260714-123426-H6-inline-probe.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-122159-ac772045578314808.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-122159-aa7a4cc064596f222.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-122159-ae52507d7fc1a55b7.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-122159-a7875c1b02b7ec340.jsonl",".cq/logs/raw/20260714-122159-a971f55e45232ce3d.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T12:11:27.104Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T12:57:05.518Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"7295f080-20fa-4cf9-afac-0357b4cf65cb"}}

1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/base64"
5		"encoding/hex"
6		"errors"
7		"fmt"
8		"math"
9		"net"
10		"net/netip"
11		"strconv"
12		"strings"
13		"time"
14	
15		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
16	)
17	
18	// Role selects which end of the tunnel this process runs as. It is an explicit,
19	// required field; the role is never inferred from other configuration.
20	type Role string
21	
22	const (
23		// RoleEdge is the mobile Linux box that bonds the WAN uplinks.
24		RoleEdge Role = "edge"
25		// RoleConcentrator is the public-IP VPS that terminates the tunnel.
26		RoleConcentrator Role = "concentrator"
27	)
28	
29	func (r Role) valid() bool {
30		return r == RoleEdge || r == RoleConcentrator
31	}
32	
33	// keyLen is the byte length of a Curve25519 WireGuard key and of a raw
34	// outer-control PSK, before base64 encoding.
35	const keyLen = 32
36	
37	// Config is the whole wanbond configuration, shared by both roles and parsed
38	// from a single TOML file.
39	type Config struct {
40		Role      Role            `toml:"role"`
41		Paths     []Path          `toml:"paths"`
42		WireGuard WireGuard       `toml:"wireguard"`
43		Amnezia   Amnezia         `toml:"amnezia"`
44		PSK       Key             `toml:"psk"`
45		Metrics   Metrics         `toml:"metrics"`
46		Log       Log             `toml:"log"`
47		Scheduler SchedulerConfig `toml:"scheduler"`
48		FEC       FEC             `toml:"fec"`
49		DNS       DNS             `toml:"dns"`
50		// Bind is the OPTIONAL top-level default bind mode (I5, Q42) applied to every
51		// path that omits its own `bind`. Left empty it defaults to BindModeAuto
52		// (today's selectDeviceBinds heuristic) in normalize(), so an existing config
53		// with no `bind` anywhere keeps exactly today's per-path bind behaviour.
54		Bind BindMode `toml:"bind"`
55		// TUNPersist opts wanbond0 into surviving daemon restarts (I7, Q38). Default
56		// false keeps today's teardown semantics exactly: the TUN is non-persistent
57		// and the kernel destroys it when the daemon's last fd closes on Close, so
58		// addresses/routes/rules referencing it are dropped on every restart. Set true
59		// and the daemon issues TUNSETPERSIST on start (device.Up) — the link then
60		// outlives Close (amneziawg-go's NativeTun.Close only closes the fd/netlink
61		// socket; it never issues RTM_DELLINK), and the next start re-adopts the same
62		// persistent device BY NAME via CreateTUN's TUNSETIFF, preserving its ifindex
63		// so operator-owned addressing survives untouched. On an NM host a persistent
64		// device STILL needs the unmanaged-devices drop-in (D39) — persistence keeps
65		// the link across restarts but does not exempt it from NetworkManager.
66		TUNPersist bool `toml:"tun_persist"`
67	}
68	
69	// BindMode selects, per path, how that path's UDP socket is bound to the
70	// network at Open time (I5, Q42):
71	//
72	//   - BindModeAuto reproduces today's selectDeviceBinds heuristic: device-bind
73	//     (SO_BINDTODEVICE) only when provably equivalent to pinning source_addr,
74	//     source-IP-bind otherwise.
75	//   - BindModeSource forces the pre-T16 source-IP pin unconditionally.
76	//   - BindModeDevice forces a device bind unconditionally.
77	type BindMode string
78	
79	const (
80		// BindModeSource forces the source-IP pin (pre-T16 behaviour).
81		BindModeSource BindMode = "source"
82		// BindModeDevice forces a device bind (SO_BINDTODEVICE).
83		BindModeDevice BindMode = "device"
84		// BindModeAuto reproduces today's selectDeviceBinds heuristic. It is the
85		// default when a path (and the top-level default) both omit `bind`.
86		BindModeAuto BindMode = "auto"
87	)
88	
89	func (b BindMode) valid() bool {
90		return b == BindModeSource || b == BindModeDevice || b == BindModeAuto
91	}
92	
93	// SchedulerPolicy selects the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind
94	// runs. It is a bounded enum: the P1 active-backup failover (default) or the T21
95	// weighted-aggregation policy. The policy is an explicit config choice — active-
96	// backup stays selectable so the P1 behaviour is never removed, only extended.
97	type SchedulerPolicy string
98	
99	const (
100		// PolicyActiveBackup is the P1 single-active-path failover scheduler: all egress
101		// on the highest-priority live path, every other path idle (data-thrift by
102		// construction). It is the default when [scheduler] is omitted.
103		PolicyActiveBackup SchedulerPolicy = "active-backup"
104		// PolicyWeighted is the T21 weighted-aggregation scheduler: under load a single
105		// flow is striped across both paths in proportion to per-path RTT/loss-derived
106		// weight, collapsing to the primary at low load (5G stays ~idle) with hysteresis,
107		// and per-path send-pacing to bound bufferbloat.
108		PolicyWeighted SchedulerPolicy = "weighted"
109	)
110	
111	func (p SchedulerPolicy) valid() bool {
112		return p == PolicyActiveBackup || p == PolicyWeighted
113	}
114	
115	// Weighted-aggregation policy defaults (T21). They are applied when [scheduler]
116	// selects the weighted policy but leaves a knob at its zero value, so a minimal
117	// `policy = "weighted"` block is usable without hand-tuning every threshold. They
118	// are conservative bring-up values; the empirical per-path pace is sized from a
119	// measured BDP in a bandwidth-capped fixture (P0 findings §7, deferred to T35/T23),
120	// so the shipped default leaves pacing DISABLED and only bounds distribution.
121	const (
122		// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity, in frame-selection
123		// slots per second, that the aggregation load-gate compares offered load against
124		// AND (when pacing is enabled) the per-path token-bucket refill rate. It is a
125		// synthetic proxy for path capacity: there is no measured BDP (P0 §7), so capacity
126		// is expressed in the only unit the scheduler sees — Pick() invocations per second.
127		defaultPerPathCapacityFPS = 10000.0
128		// defaultEngageFraction engages aggregation once offered load exceeds this
129		// fraction of one path's capacity — i.e. a single path is nearly saturated.
130		defaultEngageFraction = 0.9
131		// defaultDisengageFraction collapses back to primary-only once offered load falls
132		// below this fraction of one path's capacity. It is strictly below the engage
133		// fraction: the gap is the hysteresis band that stops the 5G path from dribbling
134		// on/off around a single threshold (requirement 2, data-thrift).
135		defaultDisengageFraction = 0.5
136		// defaultCollapseDwell is how long offered load must stay continuously below the
137		// disengage fraction before aggregation collapses to primary-only — the temporal
138		// half of the hysteresis, mirroring the active-backup failback dwell so a brief
139		// lull does not repeatedly park then re-engage the metered path.
140		defaultCollapseDwell = 2 * time.Second
141		// defaultLoadTau is the time constant of the exponentially-weighted offered-load
142		// rate estimator. It smooths the instantaneous Pick() arrival rate so the gate
143		// reacts to sustained load, not single-frame bursts.
144		defaultLoadTau = 200 * time.Millisecond
145		// defaultPacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst (in frame slots)
146		// when pacing is enabled: the bucket admits up to this many frames instantaneously
147		// before the refill rate binds, absorbing normal jitter without inflating a queue.
148		defaultPacingBurstFrames = 64.0
149		// defaultWeightRTTFloor floors the RTT in the weight formula so a path reporting a
150		// near-zero RTT (cold estimator, no samples yet) cannot be handed unbounded weight.
151		defaultWeightRTTFloor = 1 * time.Millisecond
152		// defaultWeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root so a zero-loss
153		// path gets a large-but-finite weight and two zero-loss paths split by inverse-RTT
154		// alone rather than both diverging.
155		defaultWeightLossFloor = 1e-3
156	)
157	
158	// SchedulerConfig selects and tunes the send scheduler. When the [scheduler] block
159	// is omitted the policy defaults to active-backup and every weighted knob is
160	// ignored, so existing configs keep the P1 behaviour unchanged. The weighted knobs
161	// are validated (and defaulted) only when the weighted policy is selected.
162	type SchedulerConfig struct {
163		// Policy selects the scheduler; defaults to active-backup when empty.
164		Policy SchedulerPolicy `toml:"policy"`
165	
166		// PerPathCapacityFPS is the reference per-path capacity in frame-selection slots
167		// per second: the denominator the aggregation load-gate compares offered load
168		// against, and the per-path pacing refill rate when PacingEnabled. Must be > 0
169		// under the weighted policy.
170		PerPathCapacityFPS float64 `toml:"per_path_capacity_fps"`
171		// EngageFraction engages aggregation when offered load exceeds
172		// EngageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS. Must be in (0,1].
173		EngageFraction float64 `toml:"engage_fraction"`
174		// DisengageFraction collapses aggregation to primary-only once offered load stays
175		// below DisengageFraction*PerPathCapacityFPS for CollapseDwell. Must be in
176		// [0,EngageFraction) — strictly below EngageFraction so the two form a hysteresis
177		// band.
178		DisengageFraction float64 `toml:"disengage_fraction"`
179		// CollapseDwell is the sustained-low-load dwell before collapsing to primary-only
180		// (hysteresis). Must be >= 0. Parsed from CollapseDwellRaw in normalize.
181		CollapseDwell time.Duration `toml:"-"`
182		// CollapseDwellRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of CollapseDwell, e.g.
183		// "2s" (D43). Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast, mirroring
184		// Path.LinkRTTRaw.
185		CollapseDwellRaw string `toml:"collapse_dwell"`
186		// LoadTau is the offered-load rate estimator's time constant. Must be > 0. Parsed
187		// from LoadTauRaw in normalize.
188		LoadTau time.Duration `toml:"-"`
189		// LoadTauRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of LoadTau, e.g. "200ms" (D43).
190		// Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast.
191		LoadTauRaw string `toml:"load_tau"`
192	
193		// PacingEnabled turns per-path send-pacing on. When false the token buckets are
194		// bypassed (a documented no-op — P0 §7 could not empirically size the pace in the
195		// unmetered fixture), but PerPathCapacityFPS still drives the aggregation gate.
196		PacingEnabled bool `toml:"pacing_enabled"`
197		// PacingBurstFrames is the per-path token-bucket burst in frame slots. Must be > 0
198		// when PacingEnabled.
199		PacingBurstFrames float64 `toml:"pacing_burst_frames"`
200	
201		// WeightRTTFloor floors RTT in the weight formula (must be > 0 under weighted).
202		// Parsed from WeightRTTFloorRaw in normalize.
203		WeightRTTFloor time.Duration `toml:"-"`
204		// WeightRTTFloorRaw is the TOML Go-duration string form of WeightRTTFloor, e.g.
205		// "1ms" (D43). Parsed in normalize; an unparseable value fails fast.
206		WeightRTTFloorRaw string `toml:"weight_rtt_floor"`
207		// WeightLossFloor floors the loss term under the square root (must be > 0 under
208		// weighted).
209		WeightLossFloor float64 `toml:"weight_loss_floor"`
210	}
211	
212	// BDPSizing is a per-path pacing sizing derived from a MEASURED path, in the
213	// frame-selection-slot units the weighted scheduler pace uses (defect D22). It is the
214	// empirical alternative to the synthetic frame-count defaults: CapacityFPS replaces
215	// PerPathCapacityFPS and BurstFrames replaces PacingBurstFrames.
216	type BDPSizing struct {
217		// CapacityFPS is the sustained per-path frame rate the measured bottleneck bandwidth
218		// supports (the token-bucket refill rate and aggregation-gate denominator).
219		CapacityFPS float64
220		// BurstFrames is the bandwidth-delay product expressed in frames (bandwidth x RTT,
221		// i.e. one RTT of in-flight frames): the token-bucket burst that absorbs one RTT of
222		// jitter without building a standing queue.
223		BurstFrames float64
224	}
225	
226	// defaultAvgWireFrameBytes is the conservative average on-wire outer-frame size used
227	// when deriving the per-path pace from an operator-declared bandwidth at config load
228	// (T53): a full IPv4 path MTU, since a full-MTU DATA datagram occupies about one path
229	// MTU on the wire. It mirrors bind.DefaultPathMTU (1500) rather than importing it —
230	// internal/bind imports internal/config, so config cannot import bind without a cycle
231	// (the same mirror-with-cross-reference pattern as maxFECDeadline above). Sizing
232	// capacity with the full-MTU frame is the conservative floor: smaller average frames
233	// would yield a HIGHER frame rate, so this never over-paces a path.
234	const defaultAvgWireFrameBytes = 1500.0
235	
236	// SizePacingFromBDP derives the weighted scheduler's per-path pacing parameters from a
237	// measured path instead of the synthetic frame-count default (defect D22). The shipped
238	// defaultPerPathCapacityFPS (10000, ~115 Mbit/s at full MTU) sits far above a realistic
239	// slow uplink, so the aggregation gate may never engage and — with pacing enabled — the
240	// pace never binds; deriving capacity from the measured bottleneck bandwidth fixes both.
241	//
242	// capacity_fps = bandwidth / (8 * avg wire frame bytes) — frames/s the link sustains.
243	// burst_frames = capacity_fps * RTT — one RTT of in-flight frames (equivalently
244	// BDP_bytes / avg wire frame bytes, since the bandwidth-delay product BDP = bandwidth * RTT).
245	//
246	// bandwidthBitsPerSec is the measured bottleneck bandwidth (bits/s), rtt the measured
247	// path RTT, and avgWireFrameBytes the average on-wire outer-frame size (a full-MTU
248	// datagram plus frame.DataOverhead is the conservative choice). It fails fast on a
249	// non-positive input rather than emitting a nonsensical (zero/negative/Inf) sizing.
250	func SizePacingFromBDP(bandwidthBitsPerSec float64, rtt time.Duration, avgWireFrameBytes float64) (BDPSizing, error) {
251		if bandwidthBitsPerSec <= 0 {
252			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing bandwidth must be > 0 bit/s, got %g", bandwidthBitsPerSec)
253		}
254		if rtt <= 0 {
255			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing RTT must be > 0, got %s", rtt)
256		}
257		if avgWireFrameBytes <= 0 {
258			return BDPSizing{}, fmt.Errorf("config: BDP sizing average wire frame size must be > 0 bytes, got %g", avgWireFrameBytes)
259		}
260		const bitsPerByte = 8.0
261		capacityFPS := bandwidthBitsPerSec / (bitsPerByte * avgWireFrameBytes)
262		burstFrames := capacityFPS * rtt.Seconds()
263		return BDPSizing{CapacityFPS: capacityFPS, BurstFrames: burstFrames}, nil
264	}
